source_textbook: Beginning Python — From Blocks to Code with Monty

questions.question: What is this course about?
questions.answer: Beginning Python — From Blocks to Code with Monty is a project-driven course that takes students who have already learned Scratch and teaches them real Python. It starts with visual turtle graphics and builds toward variables, loops, functions, data structures, and machine learning. Monty the python-snake mascot guides every lesson.

questions.question: Who is this course for?
questions.answer: This course is for elementary and middle school students ages 10-13 who have completed at least one semester of block-based programming (Scratch, Snap!, or MIT App Inventor) and are ready to move to text-based coding. No prior Python experience required.

questions.question: What should I already know before starting?
questions.answer: You should be comfortable with sequences, loops, conditionals, and variables from Scratch or a similar block-based environment. No prior Python, no math beyond arithmetic, and no installation required.

questions.question: How do I run Python code in this textbook?
questions.answer: Every lesson in Chapters 1-18 contains Skulpt labs — interactive code windows built right into the page. Click Run and Python executes immediately in your browser. No login, no account, no installation needed.

questions.question: What is Skulpt and why do Chapters 1-18 use it?
questions.answer: Skulpt is a version of Python that runs entirely inside a web browser. Because it runs in the browser, students do not need to install anything or open a new tab. The first 18 chapters focus on concepts Skulpt handles well: print, variables, loops, functions, lists, and turtle graphics.

questions.question: Who is Monty and what does he do?
questions.answer: Monty is the friendly python-snake mascot who has exactly seven jobs: welcoming you at the start of each lesson, helping you predict what code will do, giving tips, bridging Python to Scratch, warning about common mistakes, encouraging you when concepts are hard, and celebrating at lesson end.

questions.question: Do I need to install Python to use this textbook?
questions.answer: No — not for Chapters 1-18. All you need is a modern web browser. The inline Skulpt labs run Python right on the page. For advanced chapters using NumPy, matplotlib, or Keras, you can install Python from python.org or use Google Colab.

questions.question: What makes Python different from Scratch?
questions.answer: In Scratch you drag colorful blocks. In Python you type instructions as text. The core ideas — sequences, loops, conditionals, variables — are the same. Typing gives far more flexibility: you can work with large data, build graphics, and create machine learning models.

questions.question: What can I build by the end of this course?
questions.answer: By the end of 38 chapters you can build: turtle graphics art and animated fractals, interactive games with mouse/keyboard events, data-processing programs that read files, custom reusable modules, maze solvers using graph algorithms, matplotlib dashboards, and a neural network recognizing handwritten digits.

questions.question: How long does this course take to complete?
questions.answer: Approximately one school year in a coding club or elective class meeting two or three times per week, or 30-45 minutes per session for self-directed students. Chapters 1-18 form a complete beginning track; Chapters 19-38 are intermediate-to-advanced extension.

questions.question: Is there a teacher's guide available?
questions.answer: Yes. The course includes a detailed teacher's guide with pacing recommendations, prerequisite concept maps, discussion prompts, common misconceptions, extension activities, assessment rubrics, and alignment to CSTA K-12 CS education standards.

questions.question: How are the chapters organized?
questions.answer: The 38 chapters follow a learning graph of 450 concepts. Chapters 1-5 introduce the environment and turtle graphics. Chapters 6-18 cover the core language. Chapters 19-30 add collections, error handling, OOP, and algorithms. Chapters 31-38 cover data visualization, NumPy, image processing, and machine learning.

questions.question: What is the Python interpreter?
questions.answer: The Python interpreter is the program that reads your Python code and carries out its instructions, one line at a time from top to bottom. In Skulpt, this runs inside the browser. In a terminal, the standard CPython interpreter does the same job on your computer.

questions.question: What is indentation and why does Python care about it?
questions.answer: Indentation means putting spaces at the start of a line to show it belongs inside a block. In Python, indentation is part of the language syntax — it defines loop bodies, if blocks, and function bodies. Most programmers use 4 spaces per level.

questions.question: What is a variable?
questions.answer: A variable is a named container that holds a value. You create one with the assignment operator =. After `score = 10`, the name `score` refers to the number 10. You can use the variable name anywhere you would use the value, and update it by assigning a new value.

questions.question: What are the six core Python data types?
questions.answer: The six core data types are: int (whole numbers), float (decimal numbers), str (text), bool (True/False), list (ordered changeable collection), and dict (key-value pairs). You can check any value's type with type(value).

questions.question: What is a for loop?
questions.answer: A for loop repeats a block of code once for each item in a sequence. The most common form uses range() to count: `for i in range(4):` prints 0, 1, 2, 3. You can also loop over a list, a string, or any sequence.

questions.question: What is a while loop?
questions.answer: A while loop repeats a block of code as long as a condition stays True. Unlike a for loop which counts a fixed number of repetitions, a while loop runs an unknown number of times — useful for animation, input validation, and game loops.

questions.question: How does an if/elif/else statement work?
questions.answer: An if statement runs a block only when a condition is True. elif checks additional conditions in order. else catches everything that did not match. Python checks each condition top to bottom and runs only the first block that is True.

questions.question: What is a function and why should I use one?
questions.answer: A function is a reusable block of code you give a name and call whenever needed. Functions avoid copying code (DRY principle), make programs readable, and break big problems into small pieces. Define with def, call by name.

questions.question: What is a list?
questions.answer: A list is an ordered, changeable collection of values created with square brackets: `colors = ['red', 'green', 'blue']`. Access items by index starting at 0. Add items with append(), remove with remove() or pop(), sort with sort().

questions.question: What is a dictionary?
questions.answer: A dictionary stores data as key-value pairs. Instead of a number index, you look up values by a descriptive key: `grades['Alice']`. Perfect for grade books, word frequency counters, or any named data. Keys must be unique.

questions.question: What is turtle graphics?
questions.answer: Turtle graphics is a way to draw shapes by controlling a small arrow (the 'turtle') that moves around a canvas. Commands like forward(100), left(90), and pencolor('red') direct it. It is the primary drawing tool in Chapters 5-18.

questions.question: What is recursion?
questions.answer: Recursion is when a function calls itself. Each call works on a smaller version of the problem until a base case stops the chain. Without a base case, the function calls itself forever (RecursionError). Great for fractals and tree structures.

questions.question: What is a module?
questions.answer: A module is a Python file containing reusable functions and classes. You load it with import. Python ships with a large standard library: math, random, re, os, json, and many more. You can also create and import your own modules.

questions.question: What is a class in Python?
questions.answer: A class is a blueprint for creating objects with their own attributes (data) and methods (actions). Define with class, set up attributes in __init__, and create instances by calling the class name. turtle.Turtle() is a built-in example.

questions.question: What is a string?
questions.answer: A string is a sequence of text characters in quotes. Strings are immutable — you can't change individual characters, but you can create new strings by combining or transforming existing ones using methods like upper(), split(), and replace().

questions.question: What is the difference between a list, a tuple, and a set?
questions.answer: Lists are ordered and changeable (mutable), allowing duplicates. Tuples are ordered and unchangeable (immutable), allowing duplicates. Sets are unordered and changeable, but no duplicates allowed. Use lists for ordered data, tuples for fixed data, sets for membership testing.

questions.question: What is scope in Python?
questions.answer: Scope determines where a variable can be seen. A variable defined inside a function has local scope — only visible inside that function. A variable defined outside all functions has global scope — visible everywhere. Two functions can each have their own variable named count without conflict.

questions.question: What are BFS and DFS?
questions.answer: BFS (Breadth-First Search) uses a queue to explore neighbors at the current distance before going deeper — it guarantees the shortest path. DFS (Depth-First Search) uses a stack or recursion to plunge deep before backtracking. BFS suits maze solving; DFS suits exhaustive exploration.

questions.question: What is object-oriented programming?
questions.answer: OOP organizes code around objects — entities that bundle data (attributes) and behavior (methods). Python is object-oriented: strings, lists, and turtle graphics are all objects. OOP is especially useful for modeling real-world things like players, quiz questions, or drawing agents.

questions.question: What is a graph data structure?
questions.answer: A graph is a collection of nodes connected by edges — modeling networks like cities, web pages, or maze rooms. Python represents graphs as adjacency lists: dictionaries where each key is a node and its value is a list of neighbors. BFS and DFS search graphs.

questions.question: What is the random module?
questions.answer: The random module generates random numbers and makes random choices. Key functions: random.randint(a,b) — random integer; random.choice(seq) — pick from a list; random.random() — float between 0 and 1; random.shuffle(lst) — shuffle a list in place.

questions.question: What is machine learning?
questions.answer: Machine learning is a branch of AI where a program learns patterns from labeled data instead of following hand-written rules. This course uses Keras to build a neural network that learns to recognize handwritten digits from the MNIST dataset (60,000 training images).

questions.question: What is a list comprehension?
questions.answer: A list comprehension is a compact one-line way to build a new list: `[x**2 for x in range(10)]` creates a list of squares. You can add an optional if filter: `[x for x in range(20) if x % 2 == 0]` creates a list of even numbers.

questions.question: What is the difference between mutable and immutable types?
questions.answer: A mutable type can be changed after creation — lists and dictionaries are mutable, so you can add, remove, or update their contents. An immutable type cannot be changed — strings and tuples are immutable; any 'change' creates a new object instead.

questions.question: What is event-driven programming?
questions.answer: Event-driven programming is where the program waits for events — mouse clicks, key presses — and runs a callback function when each event occurs. Instead of executing top-to-bottom once, the program enters an event loop. Turtle's onscreenclick() is a built-in example.

questions.question: What is the difference between Python 2 and Python 3?
questions.answer: Python 3 (2008) made incompatible improvements. The biggest practical difference: print changed from a statement to a function, and division of two integers now returns a float. This course uses Python 3. Python 2 reached end-of-life in January 2020.

questions.question: What does the print() function do?
questions.answer: print() displays output to the screen. Pass one or more comma-separated values and Python prints them on one line with spaces between, followed by a newline. It is also the most fundamental debugging tool: add print() calls to show variable values when something goes wrong.

questions.question: How do f-strings work?
questions.answer: An f-string lets you embed variable values directly in a string by placing f before the opening quote and wrapping variable names in {}: `f'Hi {name}, you scored {score} points.'` Any Python expression inside {} is evaluated and inserted. F-strings are the preferred modern Python formatting method.

questions.question: What does the range() function do?
questions.answer: range(n) generates integers from 0 up to but not including n. With three arguments: range(start, stop, step). range(5,0,-1) counts down. Range does not create a list in memory — it generates values one at a time, making it efficient for large counts.

questions.question: What is the modulo operator?
questions.answer: The modulo operator % returns the remainder after division. `10 % 3` is 1. Most common uses: testing even/odd (`if n % 2 == 0`) and printing every Nth iteration inside a loop.

questions.question: How do I get input from the user?
questions.answer: The built-in input() function prints a prompt and waits for the user to type. It always returns a string, so convert to int or float if needed: `age = int(input('How old are you? '))`. Wrap in try/except to handle non-numeric input.

questions.question: What is the difference between = and ==?
questions.answer: = is the assignment operator — it stores a value in a variable. == is the equality comparison operator — it tests whether two values are equal and returns True or False. Using = inside an if condition is a SyntaxError in Python (unlike C or Java).

questions.question: What are comparison operators?
questions.answer: Python's comparison operators return True or False: == (equal), != (not equal), < (less than), > (greater than), <= (less than or equal), >= (greater than or equal). Combine with logical operators and, or, not.

questions.question: What does the import statement do?
questions.answer: import loads a module and makes its contents available under the module's name. You can also import specific names (from math import sqrt) or create an alias (import numpy as np). Python runs the module's code once and caches it, so repeated imports are efficient.

questions.question: What is the math module?
questions.answer: The math module provides mathematical functions: math.sqrt(x), math.pi, math.sin(x), math.cos(x), math.radians(), math.floor(), math.ceil(), math.factorial(n). It is used heavily in Chapter 18 for plotting sine waves and other mathematical turtle graphics.

questions.question: What is a traceback?
questions.answer: A traceback is Python's error report printed when a runtime error occurs. It shows the chain of function calls (most recent last), then the error type and message. Read bottom-to-top: the last line tells what went wrong; lines above tell where.
concepts: Syntax vs Runtime vs Logic Errors

questions.question: What is a try/except block?
questions.answer: A try/except block lets your program handle errors gracefully instead of crashing. Code in try runs normally; if a specified error occurs, Python jumps to except. Catch the most specific exception possible — bare except makes bugs harder to find.

questions.question: How do I read a file in Python?
questions.answer: Use open() with a with statement. The with statement automatically closes the file even if an error occurs: `with open('file.txt', 'r') as f: for line in f: print(line.strip())`. Mode 'r' reads, 'w' writes (overwrites), 'a' appends.
concepts: File Modes r w a b

questions.question: What is a regular expression?
questions.answer: A regular expression (regex) is a pattern describing a set of strings. The re module lets you search, extract, and replace text: `re.findall(r'\d{3}-\d{4}', text)` finds all phone numbers. Common pieces: \d (digit), \w (word char), + (one or more), * (zero or more).

questions.question: What are the turtle movement commands?
questions.answer: Core turtle movement commands: forward(n) moves n pixels, backward(n) moves backwards, left(angle) turns left, right(angle) turns right. Supporting: penup()/pendown() lift/lower pen, goto(x,y) teleports, speed(n) controls drawing speed (0 is fastest).

questions.question: What is NumPy?
questions.answer: NumPy is a Python library for fast numerical computing. Its ndarray supports element-wise math far faster than Python lists: `np.array([1,2,3]) * 2` gives `[2 4 6]`. NumPy is the foundation of scientific Python and is used by matplotlib and Keras.

questions.question: What is matplotlib?
questions.answer: matplotlib is the most widely-used Python data visualization library. matplotlib.pyplot provides line plots, scatter plots, bar charts, and histograms. Use plt.plot(), plt.title(), plt.xlabel(), plt.ylabel(), and plt.show() to create a labeled line chart.

questions.question: What does lambda mean?
questions.answer: A lambda is a short, anonymous function written in one expression: `square = lambda x: x**2`. Useful when you need a simple function for only one place. Commonly passed to sorted() and map(): `sorted(words, key=lambda w: len(w))` sorts by word length.

questions.question: Why does Python say IndentationError?
questions.answer: IndentationError means a line is indented inconsistently. Common causes: mixing tabs and spaces (Python 3 treats them differently), wrong indent level, or forgetting to indent a line that should be inside a block. Fix: use 4 spaces everywhere and configure your editor to always insert spaces.
concepts: Syntax vs Runtime vs Logic Errors

questions.question: Why is my turtle not drawing anything?
questions.answer: Common causes: program ended before canvas displayed (call turtle.done()), pen is up (call t.pendown()), turtle color matches background, or forward(0) draws nothing. In Skulpt, the output appears in the page div below the editor, not a separate window.

questions.question: What is the difference between a syntax error and a runtime error?
questions.answer: A syntax error is detected before the program runs — Python cannot parse the code. A runtime error (exception) is detected while the program runs — the code is valid but something goes wrong. Logic errors run silently but produce wrong answers; Python gives no message.
concepts: Syntax vs Runtime vs Logic Errors

questions.question: Why does my while loop run forever?
questions.answer: An infinite loop happens when the condition never becomes False. Most common causes: forgetting to update the loop variable, an off-by-one in the update (count += 0 instead of count += 1), or a condition that can never be False. Add print(count) inside to verify the value is changing.

questions.question: Why do I get a NameError for a variable I defined?
questions.answer: NameError means Python cannot find the variable in scope. Common causes: typo (Python is case-sensitive), variable defined inside a function (local scope), variable used before it is assigned, or loop variable used after an empty loop.

questions.question: What is RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded?
questions.answer: This error means a recursive function called itself so many times Python ran out of call stack space (default limit: 1000). Causes: missing base case, or recursive call that doesn't reduce the problem toward the base case. Add print(n) at the top to verify the value is decreasing.

questions.question: Why does my function return None instead of a value?
questions.answer: Every Python function returns None unless you explicitly use return with a value. Common causes: forgot the return statement, return is inside an if block that was not reached, or used print() (which displays but doesn't return) instead of return.

questions.question: What does TypeError: can only concatenate str (not 'int') to str mean?
questions.answer: This means you tried to join a string and a number with +. Fix: convert the number with str(): `'I am ' + str(age) + ' years old.'` Or better, use an f-string: `f'I am {age} years old.'`

questions.question: Why does my for loop skip the last number?
questions.answer: Because range(n) generates numbers up to but not including n. range(5) gives 0,1,2,3,4 — it stops at 4, not 5. To include n, use range(n+1). To loop 1 through 10 inclusive: range(1, 11).

questions.question: How do I fix a logic error when there is no error message?
questions.answer: Strategies: (1) add print statements to show variable values at key points, (2) trace the code by hand on paper, (3) test smaller pieces independently to isolate the problem, (4) rubber-duck debug — explain your code out loud, which often reveals the mistake.
concepts: Syntax vs Runtime vs Logic Errors

questions.question: Why does 1 == 1.0 return True in Python?
questions.answer: Python compares int and float values mathematically: 1 == 1.0 is True because the number one is the same regardless of type. However, 1 is 1.0 is False because is checks object identity, not mathematical equality. Use == for value comparisons; use is only for None, True, False.

questions.question: How should I name my variables?
questions.answer: Variable names should be descriptive (student_count not n), use snake_case (words separated by underscores), be lowercase (only constants use ALL_CAPS), and not shadow Python keywords (avoid list, type, input). Good naming makes code read almost like English.

questions.question: When should I write a function instead of repeating code?
questions.answer: Write a function when you copy and paste the same code more than twice, when a block grows too long to follow easily, when you want to name a complex operation, or when you want to test a piece of logic independently. Functions make bugs easier to fix — repair once instead of hunting copies.

questions.question: How do I choose between a list and a dictionary?
questions.answer: Use a list when items are ordered and accessed by position. Use a dictionary when you need to look up values by a meaningful name/key. If you find yourself writing 'if item in list' repeatedly for lookup, switch to a dictionary or set for efficiency.

questions.question: What is the DRY principle?
questions.answer: DRY stands for Don't Repeat Yourself. Every piece of logic should exist in exactly one place. When you copy and paste code and later need to change it, you risk forgetting to update a copy. Functions, modules, and loops are all tools for avoiding repetition.

questions.question: When should I use a for loop versus a while loop?
questions.answer: Use a for loop when you know in advance how many times to repeat — iterating a list, counting from 1 to 10. Use a while loop when repetitions depend on something that changes during execution — waiting for input, running an animation, or searching until found.

questions.question: How do I make my code easier to read?
questions.answer: Use meaningful names, keep functions short (under 20 lines), add blank lines between logical sections, use constants for magic numbers, write docstrings for functions, and avoid nesting more than 3 levels deep. Clean code rarely needs comments because the names explain everything.

questions.question: When should I use try/except?
questions.answer: Use try/except at system boundaries where external things can go wrong unpredictably: converting user input to a number, opening a file that might not exist, parsing external data. Do NOT use it to hide bugs in your own logic — those are errors to fix, not silence.

questions.question: When is recursion a good choice?
questions.answer: Recursion shines when the problem is naturally self-similar: fractal drawings, tree traversal, maze solving with backtracking, or directory listing. For simple counting problems, a for or while loop is usually simpler. Deep recursion uses more memory — Python's default limit is 1000 calls.

questions.question: How do I organize a larger Python program?
questions.answer: Break work into functions (one job each), group related functions into modules, use a main() function as entry point with the `if __name__ == '__main__':` guard, keep data separate from display logic, and write a short docstring at the top of each file.

questions.question: How do I use version control with Python?
questions.answer: Git is the standard tool. Basic workflow: `git init` (setup), `git add file.py` (stage), `git commit -m 'message'` (snapshot), `git log` (history). Version control lets you experiment safely — roll back to any working snapshot. GitHub is the most popular hosting service.

questions.question: How does BFS guarantee the shortest path?
questions.answer: BFS explores nodes level by level using a queue (FIFO). It processes all nodes at distance 1 before any at distance 2. The first time BFS reaches the goal, it got there via the fewest steps — a longer path cannot arrive first because the shorter path was fully explored earlier.

questions.question: What is a convolutional neural network?
questions.answer: A CNN is a neural network designed for grid-structured data like images. Instead of connecting every pixel to every neuron, it uses small filters that slide across the image detecting edges, corners, and patterns locally. Stacking Conv2D and MaxPooling2D layers detects increasingly complex features.

questions.question: What is the MNIST dataset?
questions.answer: MNIST contains 70,000 grayscale images of handwritten digits 0-9, each 28x28 pixels. 60,000 are used for training and 10,000 for testing. It is the 'Hello World' of machine learning — models typically achieve 97-99% accuracy, making it a good benchmark for learning how neural networks work.
concepts: Training vs Validation vs Test Sets

questions.question: What is the difference between supervised and unsupervised learning?
questions.answer: Supervised learning trains on labeled examples — each input has a correct answer provided. MNIST is supervised: every image is labeled 0-9. Unsupervised learning finds structure in unlabeled data — the model discovers groupings on its own. This course focuses on supervised learning.

questions.question: What is NumPy broadcasting?
questions.answer: Broadcasting is NumPy's rule for applying operations between arrays of different shapes. `array + 5` behaves as if 5 were an array the same shape as array, filled with fives. This makes numerical code concise and efficient without writing explicit loops.

questions.question: How does image processing work with Pillow?
questions.answer: Pillow (PIL) lets you open, display, modify, and save images. Key steps: Image.open() loads the file, image.show() displays it, image.resize() changes dimensions, image.convert('L') converts to grayscale, image.save() writes the result. Pixel data can be accessed as a NumPy array for per-pixel operations.

questions.question: How do I create my own Python module?
questions.answer: Save Python functions in a .py file (e.g., shapes.py). Any function defined there is available when another program does `import shapes`. Both files must be in the same directory. Add `if __name__ == '__main__':` guard to include test code that runs only when the file is executed directly.

questions.question: What are common sorting algorithms and when do I need them?
questions.answer: This course covers bubble sort (swap adjacent out-of-order elements, O(n²)), selection sort (find minimum and move to front, O(n²)), and insertion sort (insert each element in correct position). In practice, use Python's built-in sorted() which uses Timsort (O(n log n)). You study them to understand Big-O and algorithm analysis.

questions.question: What is abstraction in programming?
questions.answer: Abstraction means hiding complexity behind a simple interface. When you call t.forward(100), you do not need to know how the turtle library draws pixels — you just give a distance. Functions, modules, and classes are all forms of abstraction that let you think at a higher level without managing low-level details.

questions.question: What is object inheritance?
questions.answer: Inheritance lets a child class reuse all the attributes and methods of a parent class, adding or overriding only what it needs. Use `class Dog(Animal):` to inherit from Animal. `super().__init__()` calls the parent's constructor. This avoids duplicating shared behavior across multiple classes.

questions.question: What is the json module and when should I use it?
questions.answer: The json module converts between Python objects and JSON text. json.dumps() converts a Python dict or list to a JSON string; json.loads() parses a JSON string back to Python. json.dump() and json.load() read/write JSON directly to/from files. Use JSON for config files, saving game state, or exchanging data with web APIs.

questions.question: What is overfitting in machine learning?
questions.answer: Overfitting happens when a model learns the training data so well that it performs poorly on new data it hasn't seen. It memorizes noise instead of learning the underlying pattern. Signs: very high training accuracy but much lower test accuracy. Remedies include dropout regularization, more training data, and simpler models.
concepts: Training vs Validation vs Test Sets

questions.question: What is decomposition in computational thinking?
questions.answer: Decomposition is breaking a large problem into smaller, more manageable sub-problems. Instead of trying to write a maze solver all at once, you first write a function to read the maze, then one to find neighbors, then one to run BFS, then one to display the solution. Each piece is easier to write and test independently.
